member.office_address: 1221 Longworth House Office Building Washington DC 20515-3302

form_analysis.form_instructions_text: Instructions: Please fill out one form per request. If you have a programmatic request and an accompanying language request, please fill out this form separately for each request. Please provide detailed information regarding the location of your request in the subcommittee bill or report, down to the activity or account level. This form is only for programmatic, bill language, and report language requests. Requests submitted with incorrect or incomplete information will not be considered. A community project funding request submitted through this form will not be considered. Entities interested in community project funding must apply by completing the forms available on the Congresswoman's website. The deadline to submit a request through this form is 12:00PM on Friday March 6, 2026.

fields.label: Requesting Entity
fields.help_text: The name of the organization or group making the request.

fields.label: Point of Contact
fields.help_text: Name of the individual to contact regarding this request.

fields.label: Request Type
fields.rationale: The pre-computed reference values explicitly categorize this as a programmatic funding request for NASA's Science Mission Directorate.

fields.label: Is there a North Carolina or NC-02 specific connection to this submission? If Yes, please elaborate. (Your answer will not affect submission status)
fields.draft_value: Yes. NASA Science has invested $2,425,534.44 in NC-02 since 2023. Top grant recipients in the district include North Carolina State University and Fathom Science, Inc., supporting critical research in global flood mapping, climate injustice, and planetary science.
fields.rationale: The draft_value cites the total spending ($2,425,534.44) and specific grant totals for NC State University ($4,511,376.64) provided in the NASA spending context for NC-02.

fields.label: Has this been requested before in years past? If so, which Fiscal Year?
fields.rationale: This is a user-specific history question that cannot be determined from the provided strategic or local context.

fields.label: If this program has been previously funded, what was the most recent enacted funding level? If this is a bill or report language request, enter "N/A".
fields.help_text: Enter the numeric dollar amount from the most recently enacted budget.
fields.rationale: This is the enacted funding level for NASA's Science Mission Directorate for FY2026 as per H.R. 6938.

fields.label: Which other House and Senate Members are receiving this same request?
fields.draft_value:
* Reps. Don Bacon (R-NE) and Judy Chu (D-CA) (House letter)
* Bipartisan members of the House CJS Appropriations Subcommittee
fields.rationale: The request is part of a coordinated effort led by Reps. Bacon and Chu in the House.

fields.label: Subcommittee
fields.help_text: Select the relevant House Appropriations Subcommittee.
options: Agriculture, Rural Development, Food and Drug Administration and Related Agencies
Commerce, Justice, Science and Related Agencies
Energy and Water Development and Related Agencies
Labor, Health and Human Services, Education and Related Agencies
Military Construction, Veterans Affairs and Related Agencies
State, Foreign Operations and Related Agencies
Transportation, Housing and Urban Development and Related Agencies
fields.draft_value: Commerce, Justice, Science and Related Agencies
fields.rationale: NASA's Science Mission Directorate is funded through the Commerce, Justice, Science and Related Agencies (CJS) subcommittee as specified in the reference values.

sections.description: Information required for Programmatic Funding requests. Enter N/A if submitting a Language request.

fields.label:
Programmatic Request - Agency/Bureau/Office/Program Title (e.g. DOJ/Office of Justice Programs/State and Local Justice Assistance/Sexual Assault Forensic Exam Program Grants)
If this is a bill or report language request, enter "N/A"
fields.rationale: Standard programmatic naming convention using the Agency and Account Full Name from the reference values.

fields.label:
Description of Programmatic Request (maximum of 150 words) 
If this is a bill or report language request, enter "N/A"
fields.draft_value:
* $9 billion for NASA's Science Mission Directorate to advance Decadal Survey priorities and restore organizational stability after FY2026 workforce disruptions
* Supports critical research in NC-2, including $4.5 million in recent grants to North Carolina State University for global flood mapping and climate justice modeling
* Sustains U.S. leadership in the space race with China by funding missions to every major body in the Solar System
* Drives a 3:1 economic return on investment, fueling a STEM talent pipeline that includes Raleigh-based firms like Ecsi Fibrotools and Fathom Science
fields.rationale: Focuses on the $9B ask, cites specific NC-2 impact at NC State and local firms, and aligns with strategic advocacy points regarding workforce and international competition.

fields.label:
Programmatic Request - Funding Level
If this is a bill or report language request, enter "N/A"
options: Specific funding amount (enter amount in question below)
fields.draft_value: Specific funding amount (enter amount in question below)
fields.rationale: The request specifies a target funding level of $9,000,000,000 for NASA Science, which represents a $1.750 billion increase over the FY2026 enacted level.

fields.label:
Programmatic Request - Specific Funding Amount
If this is a bill or report language request, enter "N/A"
fields.rationale: This matches the exact Funding Ask provided in the reference values for the Science Mission Directorate.

sections.description: Information required for Bill or Report Language requests. Enter N/A if submitting a Programmatic request.

fields.label:
Language Request - Agency/Office/Program Title (e.g. DOJ/Office of Justice Programs/State and Local Justice Assistance/Sexual Assault Forensic Exam Program Grants)
If this is a programmatic request, enter "N/A"
fields.rationale: The request context specifies this is a programmatic funding request and that report/bill language is N/A.

fields.label:
Exact Language Request
If this is a programmatic request, enter "N/A"
fields.rationale: This is a programmatic funding request, not a language request.

fields.label:
Justification for Language Request (maximum of 150 words)
If this is a programmatic request, enter "N/A"
fields.rationale: This is a programmatic funding request, not a language request.

top_grants.description: Spatiotemporal quantification of surface water and flooding is essential for research on hydrological cycles. Satellite remote sensing is the only means of monitoring these dynamics across vast areas and over time. Several regional to global surface water data sets have been developed using optical time-series  either from modis-type sensors with coarse spatial resolution but daily frequency  or based on the entire landsat archive. Despite its high spatial resolution  the 16-day repeat frequency of landsat means that short lived hazardous flooding and the maximum extent of large floods are likely missed. Meanwhile  spatially coarser modis-type sensors may miss small water bodies and floods entirely. In addition  two limitations when mapping inundation with optical data have been detecting water under vegetation and cloud obscuration  which often coincides with floods. Both issues can be overcome by fusing multiple optical with Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data  taking advantage of complementary observation properties including Sar S ability to penetrate through clouds. Thus  combining observations and spectral properties of the newly available sentinel 1 Sar (S1) and sentinel 2 (S2) series of satellites with landsat 8 (L8) holds promise for global surface water and flood mapping with improved spatial and temporal resolution and accuracy. To accurately capture maximum extent of all floods in near real time  the key objectives are to (1) map flooding dynamics globally  using machine learning applied to time-series of multi-sensor optical (L8  S2) and Radar (S1) time series data  (2) assess the accuracy of the mapped flood extent  and (3) test the ability of our algorithms to map (A) ephemeral floods in a dynamic dryland river system (B) a complex delta including inundated vegetation in western canada (leveraging field validation data on extent of inundated vegetation collected during NASA S arctic boreal vulnerability experiment)  (C) extreme flooding in north carolina (during hurricanes in 2016  2018 and 2019)  and (D) small water bodies (< 5HA) in irrigated areas (i.E. Arkansas  the u.S. State with the 3rd largest irrigated area  where hundreds of small reservoirs have been constructed since this proposal is significant to this NASA solicitation as it will enable improved quantification of flood extent dynamics and water quantity. The algorithms and maps produced can be used for better mapping of floods during hazardous conditions and assessment of how changes in land cover and land use and climate impact surface water and flood dynamics.2015).
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Fusing satellite data for accurate, near real-time global flood mapping.

top_grants.description: It is flooding more than we know climate injustice from floods due to sea level rise
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Understanding climate injustice related to sea level rise flooding

top_grants.description: The first reconnaissance of Mercury was carried out by the mariner 10 spacecraft in the 1970s. Three flybys of the innermost planet yielded images of just under half of the globe at a variety of viewing geometries and resolutions. These images were used as the basis for a series of Mercury quadrangle maps; however  the map authors did not follow a uniform set of mapping conventions or units  making it difficult to compare the geology of different parts of the imaged hemisphere or to interpret boundaries between the maps. The Mercury surface  space environment  geochemistry  and ranging (MESSENGER) spacecraft orbited Mercury during 2011 15 and imaged the planet in its entirety with the Mercury Dual Imaging System (MDIS). Messenger image data products generated during the mission were used as the basis of the first global thematic map of Mercury  prepared by members of the messenger science team at 1:15 000 000 scale within a Geographical Information System (GIS). Datasets from other published and ongoing local and regional mapping efforts were incorporated into the map wherever possible albeit often subject to difference in resolution or map scales including a global map of crater and basin rims  ghost craters  smooth plains  and tectonic structures. The locations of features not resolvable at this map scale  such as pyroclastic vents and hollows  were represented by point features to assist future analyses. Impact features>90 km in diameter were also classified with a revised version of the previously established five-age classification scheme for Mercury  and their ejecta deposits mapped; below this diameter  these deposits were generally too small to be resolved at the map scale. Importantly  this thematic map and its component elements preceded the release in may 2016 of the final base maps generated from the messenger mission. These new data include the first global dem  global and regional multispectral mosaics with a new empirical calibration  and a global monochrome image mosaic base map at largely uniform viewing geometry and a resolution of ~166 m/pixel (together with two additional base maps taken under different lighting conditions). Accordingly  all previous map-based studies could not avail of the full orbital Imaging campaigns  the most complete global control network for Mercury yet developed and the first global digital elevation model  or the final calibrations of the messenger image data. Despite the immediate contextual utility of the current global thematic map  we anticipate that a global geological map of Mercury prepared to usgs standards with these new data as base maps would be of greater interest and benefit to the planetary community  and would substantially aid ongoing and future scientific analyses and exploration efforts of the planet.
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Developing standardized global geological maps of Mercury for planetary science.

top_grants.description: Understanding the drivers of the global methane increase through new sectoral inverse modeling of methane  carbon isotopic measurements  and co emitted pollutants
top_grants.generated_award_summary: Modeling global methane sources using isotopic measurements.
